content:
# Exodus Chapter 10 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~37v–38r (PDF pp. 71–72); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 50–51 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_010_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_010_page_002.jpg`

---

### The Eighth Plague: Locusts (vv. 1–20)

**1** Then YHWH said to Moses, "Go to Pharaoh — for I have made his heart heavy, and the hearts of his officials, so that I may display these signs of mine among them,

**2** and so that you may tell your children and grandchildren how I made fools of Egypt and what signs I performed among them — so that you will know that I am YHWH."

**3** So Moses and Aaron went to Pharaoh and said to him, "This is what YHWH, the God of the Hebrews, says: How long will you refuse to humble yourself before me? Let my people go, so they may serve me.

**4** Because if you refuse to let my people go, tomorrow I will bring locusts into your territory.

**5** They will cover the face of the ground so that no one will be able to see the earth. They will devour whatever escaped the hail — whatever you have left growing in the fields — and they will eat every tree that sprouts for you from the ground.

**6** They will fill your palaces, the houses of all your officials, and the houses of all the Egyptians — something neither your fathers nor your grandfathers have ever seen, from the day they first walked this land until today." Then he turned and walked out of Pharaoh's presence.

**7** Pharaoh's officials said to him, "How long will this man be a snare to us? Let the people go so they may serve YHWH their God. Do you not yet realize that Egypt is ruined?"

**8** So Moses and Aaron were brought back to Pharaoh, and he said to them, "Go, serve YHWH your God. But exactly who will be going?"

**9** Moses said, "We will go with our young and our old, with our sons and our daughters, with our flocks and our herds — because we have a festival to YHWH."

**10** Pharaoh said to them, "May YHWH be with you — if I ever let you go with your children! Clearly you have some evil scheme in mind.

**11** No! Let the men go and serve YHWH, since that is what you are asking for." And they were driven out of Pharaoh's presence.

**12** Then YHWH said to Moses, "Stretch out your hand over the land of Egypt for the locusts, and let them come up over the land of Egypt and devour every plant in the land — everything the hail has left."

**13** So Moses stretched out his staff over the land of Egypt, and YHWH drove an east wind across the land all that day and all that night. When morning came, the east wind had carried in the locusts.

**14** The locusts came up over the entire land of Egypt and settled on every part of the country in devastating numbers. Never before had there been such a locust swarm, and never again would there be one like it.

**15** They covered the face of the entire land so that the ground was black with them. They devoured every plant in the land and every fruit on the trees that the hail had left. Not a single green thing remained — no tree, no field plant — in all the land of Egypt.

**16** Pharaoh urgently summoned Moses and Aaron and said, "I have sinned against YHWH your God and against you.

**17** Now please forgive my sin just this once, and plead with YHWH your God to take this death away from me."

**18** Moses left Pharaoh's presence and pleaded with YHWH.

**19** And YHWH reversed the wind into a very strong west wind, which lifted the locusts and hurled them into the Sea of Reeds. Not a single locust remained anywhere in the territory of Egypt.

**20** But YHWH strengthened Pharaoh's heart, and he did not let the Israelites go.

### The Ninth Plague: Darkness (vv. 21–29)

**21** Then YHWH said to Moses, "Stretch out your hand toward the sky, and let there be darkness over the land of Egypt — a darkness so thick it can be touched."

**22** Moses stretched out his hand toward the sky, and a smothering darkness covered the entire land of Egypt for three days.

**23** No one could see another person, and no one could rise from where they were, for three days. But all the Israelites had light where they lived.

**24** Pharaoh summoned Moses and said, "Go, serve YHWH. Even your children may go with you. Only leave your flocks and herds behind."

**25** But Moses said, "You yourself must also provide us with sacrifices and burnt offerings so we can offer them to YHWH our God.

**26** Our livestock must go with us too — not a hoof may be left behind — because we must draw from them to serve YHWH our God, and we will not know what YHWH requires of us until we arrive there."

**27** But YHWH strengthened Pharaoh's heart, and he was not willing to let them go.

**28** Pharaoh said to him, "Get away from me! Make sure you never see my face again, because the day you see my face, you will die."

**29** Moses said, "You have spoken well. I will never see your face again."

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**vv.1-2 — THE PEDAGOGICAL PURPOSE OF THE PLAGUES (Heb. *lema'an shiti ototai elleh beqirbo... ulema'an tesapper be'oznei binkha uven-binkha*, לְמַ֗עַן שִׁתִ֛י אֹתֹתַ֥י אֵ֖לֶּה בְּקִרְבּ֑וֹ... וּלְמַ֡עַן תְּסַפֵּר֩ בְּאָזְנֵ֨י בִנְךָ֜ וּבֶן־בִּנְךָ֗):** These opening verses constitute one of the most theologically important statements in the entire plague narrative. For the first time, YHWH explicitly names *two* purposes for the plagues:

First, the display purpose: "so that I may display these signs of mine among them" — the plagues are YHWH's self-revelation within Egypt, directed at Pharaoh's heart (*beqirbo*, בקרבו, "in his midst/interior").

Second, and more strikingly, the *generational* purpose: "so that you may tell your children and grandchildren." The Hebrew *tesapper* (תספר) means "recount, narrate" — the root *spr* (ספר) from which *sefer* ("book, scroll") derives. The plagues are designed to become *story*. YHWH is not merely punishing Egypt; he is creating a narrative that Israel will transmit across generations. The verb choice is deliberate: not *zakar* ("remember") but *sapper* ("tell") — this is about active narration, not passive recollection. The plagues exist, in part, so that Israelite grandchildren will hear the story and know who YHWH is.

The verb *hit'allalti* (הִתְעַלַּ֣לְתִּי), rendered "made fools of," is from the root *'alal* (עלל, Hithpael), meaning "to deal ruthlessly with" or "to make a mockery of." The same root appears in 1 Samuel 6:6 when the Philistines recall the exodus: "Why should you harden your hearts as Egypt and Pharaoh hardened theirs? After he had made fools of them, did he not let them go?" The word carries both severity and scorn — YHWH has *played with* Egypt, exposed the impotence of its gods and the fragility of its power. The Vaticanus uses *enepaixa* (ἐνέπαιξα), "I mocked, I made sport of." This is the language of divine irony: the world's greatest empire becomes YHWH's object lesson for Israelite children.

The hardening verb in v.1 is *hikbadti* (הִכְבַּ֣דְתִּי), Hiphil of *kaved* — "I have made heavy." YHWH is now the direct agent of the *kaved*-hardening, extending his agency beyond the *chazaq*-hardening of 9:12. Both roots are now under divine control. The phrase includes Pharaoh's officials (*ve'et-lev avadav*, ואת־לב עבדיו) — the hardening has spread from the king to his court. This is the first time YHWH claims to have hardened the officials' hearts as well. The resistance is no longer one man's stubbornness; it is institutional.

**v.3 — "How long will you refuse to humble yourself before me?" (Heb. *ad-matai me'anta le'anot mippanai*, עַד־מָתַ֣י מֵאַ֔נְתָּ לֵעָנֹ֖ת מִפָּנָ֑י):** The verb *le'anot* (from *'anah*, ענה, Niphal infinitive) means "to humble oneself, to be humbled, to submit." It shares the same root as *'oni* (עני, "affliction, poverty") — the very word used to describe Israel's suffering in Egypt (3:7, *'oni ammi*, "the affliction of my people"). The demand is devastatingly ironic: YHWH asks Pharaoh to adopt the posture of the very people he has been afflicting. The oppressor must become like the oppressed — humbled, low, submissive before a greater power. The Vaticanus reads *entrepesthai* (ἐντρέπεσθαι), "to feel shame, to show respect."

The phrase *ad-matai* (עד מתי, "how long?") is a lament formula — it appears throughout the Psalms as the cry of the afflicted (Ps. 13:1-2, "How long, YHWH? Will you forget me forever?"). Here YHWH turns the formula on its head: *God* is the one asking "how long?" of the human. The lament belongs to the divine voice. YHWH experiences Pharaoh's resistance as a prolonged affront — not because God is impatient, but because every day of refusal is another day of Israelite suffering.

**v.6 — "Then he turned and walked out" (Heb. *vayyifen vayyetse me'im Par'oh*, וַיִּ֥פֶן וַיֵּצֵ֖א מֵעִ֥ם פַּרְעֹֽה):** This is a stunning moment. Moses does not wait for a response. He delivers YHWH's ultimatum — the most extreme yet, promising a locust plague surpassing anything in Egyptian memory — and simply turns his back on Pharaoh and leaves. The verb *panah* (פנה, "to turn") followed by *yatsa* (יצא, "to go out") conveys abrupt departure. There is no negotiation, no pause for Pharaoh's reaction. Moses has become the mouthpiece of a God who no longer requests but announces. The Vaticanus captures this with *apostrepsas exēlthen* (ἀποστρέψας ἐξῆλθεν), "having turned away, he went out." This walkout is what triggers the extraordinary scene that follows.

**v.7 — THE COURT FRACTURES (Heb. *ad-matai yihyeh zeh lanu lemokesh*, עַד־מָתַ֞י יִהְיֶ֨ה זֶ֥ה לָ֨נוּ֙ לְמוֹקֵ֔שׁ):** Pharaoh's own officials now turn against him — the first open break in the Egyptian power structure. Their words echo YHWH's own language from v.3: *ad-matai* ("how long?"). Where YHWH asked "how long will you refuse to humble yourself?", the officials ask "how long will this man be a snare to us?" The word *moqesh* (מוקש) means "snare, trap" — a hunter's device. From the officials' perspective, Moses is the problem: he is the trap that keeps ensnaring Egypt in catastrophe. But the narrative irony is thick: the real trap is Pharaoh's own hardened heart.

The officials' demand is remarkable: *shallach et-ha'anashim veya'avdu et-YHWH Eloheihem* — "Let the people go so they may serve YHWH *their God*." They use YHWH's name. They acknowledge YHWH's claim on Israel. They even call him "their God." And then the devastating question: *haterem teda ki avdah Mitsrayim?* (הֲטֶ֣רֶם תֵּדַ֔ע כִּ֥י אָבְדָ֖ה מִצְרָ֑יִם) — "Do you not yet realize that Egypt is ruined?" The verb *avdah* (from *abad*, אבד) means "is destroyed, is lost, has perished." Egypt is already ruined — the locusts have not yet come, but the officials see what Pharaoh cannot. The word *terem* (טרם, "not yet") echoes Moses' accusation in 9:30 (*terem tir'un*, "you do not yet fear"). Everyone can see the reality except Pharaoh. The Vaticanus preserves the force: *ouk oidas hoti apolōlen Aiguptos* (οὐκ οἶδας ὅτι ἀπόλωλεν Αἴγυπτος), "do you not know that Egypt has been destroyed?"

**vv.8-11 — PHARAOH'S NEGOTIATION: "Who exactly will be going?":** Pharaoh's question *mi vami haholkhim* (מִ֥י וָמִ֖י הַהֹלְכִ֑ים, "who and who are the ones going?") reveals his strategy. He will permit worship but control the terms — specifically, by restricting who leaves. When Moses answers with the full community (*bine'areinu uvizqeneinu... bevanenu uvivnoteinu betso'neinu uvivrqarenu*, "with our young and our old, with our sons and daughters, with our flocks and herds"), Pharaoh refuses.

Verse 10 is laden with bitter sarcasm: *yehi khen YHWH immakhem ka'asher ashalach etkhem ve'et-tappekem* (יְהִ֨י כֵ֤ן יְהוָה֙ עִמָּכֶ֔ם כַּאֲשֶׁ֛ר אֲשַׁלַּ֥ח אֶתְכֶ֖ם וְאֶת־טַפְּכֶ֑ם) — "May YHWH be with you — if I ever let you go with your children!" The invocation of YHWH's name is mocking — Pharaoh uses God's name as a curse formula, effectively saying "YHWH help you, because I never will." The word *taf* (טף) refers to the smallest children, the toddlers. Pharaoh's strategy is hostage-taking: keep the children, and the adults will have to come back.

Then: *re'u ki ra'ah neged peneikhem* (רְא֕וּ כִּ֥י רָעָ֖ה נֶ֥גֶד פְּנֵיכֶֽם) — "Look — evil is before your faces." The word *ra'ah* (רעה) means "evil, disaster, bad intent." Pharaoh accuses Moses of harboring an ulterior motive: the request to worship is a cover for escape. Ironically, Pharaoh is correct — YHWH does intend a permanent departure, not a three-day pilgrimage. But the accusation of *ra'ah* rebounds: the real evil is Pharaoh's refusal. The Vaticanus renders *blepete hoti ponēria prokeitai humin* (βλέπετε ὅτι πονηρία πρόκειται ὑμῖν), "See that evil lies before you."

Verse 11 records the expulsion: *vayyegaresh otam me'et penei Par'oh* (וַיְגָ֣רֶשׁ אֹתָ֔ם מֵאֵ֖ת פְּנֵ֥י פַרְעֹֽה) — "they were driven out from Pharaoh's presence." The verb *garash* (גרש, "to drive out, to expel") is the same verb that will be used for the final exodus itself (12:39, *goroshu miMitsrayim*, "they were driven out of Egypt"). Pharaoh drives Moses out of his court; soon YHWH will drive Israel out of Egypt entirely. The verb connects micro-expulsion to macro-exodus.

**vv.13-15 — THE LOCUST INVASION:** The east wind (*ruach qadim*, רוּחַ קָדִים) is YHWH's chosen instrument. It blows all day and all night — a sustained, deliberate act. The word *qadim* (קדים) literally means "eastern" but carries connotations of scorching dryness (cf. Hosea 13:15, Jonah 4:8). This wind comes from the desert, from the direction of Sinai. The Vaticanus uses *anemon noton* (ἄνεμον νότον), "south wind" — an interesting divergence reflecting Egyptian geography, where locust swarms more typically arrive from the south/southeast.

The description in v.14 uses emphatic language: *lifnei khen lo hayah khen arbeh kamouhu ve'acharav lo yihyeh ken* — "Before it there had never been such a locust swarm, and after it there never would be." This is nearly identical to the language used for the hail in 9:18 and anticipates Joel 2:2, where the prophet describes an eschatological locust plague in similar terms. The plagues are becoming superlative — each one exceeds all historical precedent.

Verse 15 reports total devastation: *vayekhas et-ein kol-ha'arets vattekhshakh ha'arets* — "they covered the eye of the whole land, and the land grew dark." The phrase *ein ha'arets* (עין הארץ, literally "the eye of the land") means the visible surface — the ground's "face" — but the word *'ayin* (עין, "eye") creates a visceral image: the land's eye is covered, blinded. And the verb *chashakh* (חשך, "grew dark") anticipates the ninth plague. The locusts create a proto-darkness — a preview of what is coming. The Vaticanus uses *eskotisthē hē gē* (ἐσκοτίσθη ἡ γῆ), "the earth was darkened."

**vv.16-17 — Pharaoh's second confession:** Like 9:27, Pharaoh confesses: *chatati la-YHWH Eloheikhem velakhem* — "I have sinned against YHWH your God and against you." The confession has deepened since the hail: he now includes sin against Moses and Aaron personally (*velakhem*, "and against you"), and he asks for forgiveness: *sa na chatati akh happa'am* (שָׂ֣א נָ֤א חַטָּאתִי֙ אַ֣ךְ הַפַּ֔עַם) — "Please bear/lift my sin just this once." The verb *nasa* (נשא, "to bear, to carry, to lift") is the standard term for forgiveness — God "lifts away" sin. But *akh happa'am* ("just this once") reveals the limits of the confession: Pharaoh wants a one-time reprieve, not a permanent change of heart.

Most telling is the phrase *hasiru me'alai raq et-hammavet hazzeh* (הָעְתִּ֥ירוּ... וְיָסֵר֙ מֵֽעָלַ֔י רַ֖ק אֶת־הַמָּ֥וֶת הַזֶּֽה) — "take away from me only this death." Pharaoh calls the locusts *hammavet* — "the death." Not "the plague" or "the locusts" but *death itself*. The locusts are not merely destroying crops; they are consuming Egypt's future. Without grain, Egypt dies. The Vaticanus reads *ton thanaton touton* (τὸν θάνατον τοῦτον), preserving the stark equation of locusts with death.

**v.19 — "the Sea of Reeds" (Heb. *Yam Suf*, יָ֣ם ס֑וּף):** YHWH reverses the wind — now a *ruach-yam* (רוח ים), literally "a sea-wind" or "west wind" — and it carries the locusts into the *Yam Suf*, the Sea of Reeds. This is the first mention of the Sea of Reeds in the plague narrative, and it functions as a proleptic sign: the same body of water that swallows the locusts will soon swallow Pharaoh's army (14:21-28). The wind that removes the plague foreshadows the wind that will part the sea. YHWH's mastery of wind and sea — demonstrated casually here in removing insects — will be exercised at full power in the climactic deliverance. The Vaticanus uses *thalassan eruthran* (θάλασσαν ἐρυθράν), "the Red Sea," the standard LXX translation.

**v.20 — YHWH strengthens Pharaoh's heart (Heb. *vayechazzeq YHWH et-lev Par'oh*, וַיְחַזֵּ֥ק יְהוָ֖ה אֶת־לֵ֣ב פַּרְעֹ֑ה):** The *chazaq* Piel with YHWH as agent returns — the second instance of direct divine hardening (after 9:12). The pattern is now established: after each confession, YHWH strengthens the resistance. Pharaoh's confessions in 9:27 and 10:16-17 are functionally identical — correct words, temporary terror, no lasting transformation — and each is followed by divine hardening. The Vaticanus uses *esklērunen kurios* (ἐσκλήρυνεν κύριος), the same verb Paul will cite in Romans 9:18.

**vv.21-23 — THE NINTH PLAGUE: UN-CREATION DARKNESS (Heb. *vayehi choshekh-afelah bekhol-erets Mitsrayim sheloshet yamim*, וַיְהִ֧י חֹֽשֶׁךְ־אֲפֵלָ֛ה בְּכָל־אֶ֥רֶץ מִצְרָ֖יִם שְׁלֹ֥שֶׁת יָמִֽים):** The ninth plague is the most theologically charged of all the plagues before the tenth. YHWH's instruction uses remarkable language: *veyamesh choshekh* (וְיָמֵ֖שׁ חֹֽשֶׁךְ) — "and let there be darkness that can be touched/felt." The verb *yamesh* (from *mashash*, משש) means "to grope, to feel, to touch." This is darkness with physical substance — not merely the absence of light but the *presence* of darkness. It is a darkness you can feel pressing against your skin.

The theological resonance is unmistakable. Genesis 1:2 describes the pre-creation state: *vechoshekh al-penei tehom* (וחשך על פני תהום, "and darkness was over the face of the deep"). Genesis 1:3-4 records God's first creative act: separating light from darkness. The ninth plague *reverses creation*. What God separated on Day One, he now re-merges over Egypt. The land returns to primordial chaos — the *tohu vavohu* of Genesis 1:2 descends on the empire. If the plagues have been systematically dismantling Egypt's world (water, livestock, crops, health, sky), the ninth plague dismantles *existence itself*. Egypt is being un-created.

The Hebrew *choshekh-afelah* (חשך אפלה) pairs two words for darkness: *choshekh* (the common word) and *afelah* (thick, impenetrable darkness — cf. Deut. 28:29, Joel 2:2). The doubling intensifies: this is not twilight but absolute blackness. Three days — the same duration as the proposed three-day wilderness journey (3:18, 5:3, 8:27). Egypt experiences in darkness what Israel was to experience in worship.

Verse 23 makes the Goshen distinction absolute: *ulkhol-benei Yisra'el hayah or bemoshevotam* (וּלְכָל־בְּנֵ֣י יִשְׂרָאֵ֔ל הָ֥יָה א֖וֹר בְּמוֹשְׁבֹתָֽם) — "but for all the Israelites there was light in their dwellings." Light and darkness, separated at creation, are now separated along ethnic and theological lines. The Israelites dwell in the world as God created it; the Egyptians dwell in the world as it was before God spoke. The Vaticanus reads *pasi de tois huiois Israēl ēn phōs* (πᾶσι δὲ τοῖς υἱοῖς Ισραηλ ἦν φῶς), "but for all the sons of Israel there was light." The contrast could not be more absolute: two peoples sharing the same land, one in primal darkness, the other in created light.

The immobilization is also critical: *lo ra'u ish et-achiv velo qamu ish mittachtav* (לֹא־רָא֞וּ אִ֣ישׁ אֶת־אָחִ֗יו וְלֹא־קָ֛מוּ אִ֥ישׁ מִתַּחְתָּ֖יו) — "no one could see another, and no one could rise from where they were." The darkness paralyzes. Egyptian society — its commerce, its government, its military, its daily life — stops completely. For three days, Egypt ceases to function as a civilization. This is the penultimate plague, the last warning before death itself arrives. The ninth plague says: I can unmake your world. The tenth will say: I can unmake your future.

**vv.24-26 — THE FINAL NEGOTIATION:** Pharaoh's offer represents his maximum concession: "Go, serve YHWH. Even your children may go with you. Only leave your flocks and herds behind" (*raq tso'nekhem uvqarkhem yutsag*, רַ֤ק צֹֽאנְכֶם֙ וּבְקַרְכֶ֔ם יֻצָּ֑ג). Each plague has extracted a greater concession: first nothing (ch. 7-8), then "go but stay nearby" (8:28), then "men only" (10:11), and now "everyone but the animals." Pharaoh is retreating incrementally, but he always withholds something — always retains a measure of control, a piece of leverage.

Moses' refusal is total and magnificent: *gam-attah titten beyadenu zevachim ve'olot* (גַּם־אַתָּ֛ה תִּתֵּ֥ן בְּיָדֵ֖נוּ זְבָחִ֣ים וְעֹל֑וֹת) — "You yourself must also provide us with sacrifices and burnt offerings." Not only will Israel take its own livestock — Pharaoh must *contribute* to YHWH's worship. The oppressor must fund the liberation. Then: *lo tishsha'er parsah* (לֹ֤א תִשָּׁאֵר֙ פַּרְסָ֔ה) — "not a hoof will be left behind." The word *parsah* (פרסה, "hoof") is the smallest, most irreducible unit of livestock. Moses' demand goes beyond comprehensive to absolute. Every last hoof leaves Egypt. The Vaticanus preserves the force: *ouchi katalephthēsetai hoplē* (οὐχὶ καταλειφθήσεται ὁπλή), "not a hoof will be left behind."

Verse 26 adds the theological rationale: *va'anachnu lo neda mah-na'avod et-YHWH ad-bo'enu shammah* — "we will not know what YHWH requires of us until we arrive there." This is a remarkable statement of open-ended obedience. Moses does not know in advance what the worship will require — what species, how many, which animals. He must bring everything because the requirements are not yet revealed. Worship, in Moses' theology, is not a human plan presented to God but a divine summons that will be specified at the destination. The worshiper comes with everything and waits for instruction. This anticipates the Sinai revelations, where the details of sacrifice will indeed be specified only after Israel arrives.

**v.27 — YHWH strengthens Pharaoh's heart (Heb. *vayechazzeq YHWH et-lev Par'oh*, וַיְחַזֵּ֥ק יְהוָ֖ה אֶת־לֵ֣ב פַּרְעֹ֑ה):** The third instance of direct divine *chazaq*-hardening (after 9:12, 10:20). The pattern is now unbreakable. This hardening follows the closest Pharaoh has come to full capitulation — he was willing to let everyone go, including children. Only the livestock remained. The hardening is precisely calibrated: just when Pharaoh is one concession away from release, YHWH locks the heart. The tenth plague — the one Pharaoh's stubbornness is now ensuring — requires a Pharaoh who will not yield until death enters his own house.

**vv.28-29 — THE FINAL BREAK (Heb. *heishamer lekha al-tosef re'ot panai ki beyom re'otekha fanai tamut*, הִשָּׁ֣מֶר לְךָ֗ אַל־תֹּ֨סֶף֙ רְא֣וֹת פָּנַ֔י כִּ֗י בְּי֛וֹם רְאֹתְךָ֥ פָנַ֖י תָּמֽוּת):** Pharaoh's words are a death threat: "Guard yourself — do not again see my face, because on the day you see my face, you will die." The language is ironic to its core. Pharaoh, whose land is crumbling, whose crops are devoured, whose people have sat in primordial darkness for three days, threatens *Moses* with death. The Hebrew *panai... panai* ("my face... my face") repeats for emphasis — this is about the royal *face*, the divine pretension of Pharaoh's person. To see Pharaoh's face unbidden was death in Egyptian court protocol. Pharaoh retreats to the only power he has left: courtly etiquette and the threat of execution.

Moses' response is devastating in its simplicity: *ken dibbarta lo-osif od re'ot panekha* (כֵּ֣ן דִּבַּ֔רְתָּ לֹא־אֹסִ֥ף ע֖וֹד רְא֥וֹת פָּנֶֽיךָ) — "You have spoken well. I will never see your face again." The phrase *ken dibbarta* means "you have spoken correctly, rightly, well." Moses *agrees* with Pharaoh — but the agreement carries a meaning Pharaoh does not yet understand. Moses will indeed never return to Pharaoh's court, because the next event will bring Pharaoh to *Moses*. After the tenth plague, it is Pharaoh who will summon Moses in the night (12:31), Pharaoh who will beg Israel to leave, Pharaoh whose voice will crack with the words "go, serve YHWH, as you have said." The man who says "never see my face again" will soon be the man desperate to see Moses' face one last time. The Vaticanus records: *kalōs eirēkas ouketi ophthēsomai soi eis prosōpon* — "you have spoken well; I will no longer appear before your face."

This is the last conversation between Moses and Pharaoh as equals. From this point forward, death will do the negotiating.

---

**The structural pattern of Exodus 10 — the eighth and ninth plagues completing the third cycle:**

| Plague | Cycle Position | Warning | Target | Hardening |
|--------|---------------|---------|--------|-----------|
| 8 — Locusts (vv.1-20) | Cycle 3, plague 2 | Warning to Pharaoh (extended) | Remaining crops/vegetation | *Chazaq* Piel — YHWH strengthens (v.20) |
| 9 — Darkness (vv.21-29) | Cycle 3, plague 3 | No warning | Light itself / all activity | *Chazaq* Piel — YHWH strengthens (v.27) |

The three-cycle structure is confirmed: plague 8 (cycle 3, position 2) comes with warning; plague 9 (cycle 3, position 3) arrives without warning, just as plagues 3 and 6 did. The escalation from hail (ch. 9) to locusts to darkness follows a cosmic logic: the sky attacked the earth (hail), the earth was consumed (locusts), and then the fundamental condition of life — light — was removed (darkness). The plagues have moved from individual afflictions to the undoing of creation itself.

**Hardening verb tracker for Exodus 10:**
| Verse | Hebrew Root | Stem | Agent | PNEUMA Rendering |
|-------|-----------|------|-------|-----------------|
| 10:1 | *kaved* (כבד) | Hiphil | **YHWH** (first direct divine *kaved*-hardening) | "made heavy" |
| 10:20 | *chazaq* (חזק) | Piel | **YHWH** (direct) | "strengthened" |
| 10:27 | *chazaq* (חזק) | Piel | **YHWH** (direct) | "strengthened" |

**Cumulative tracker (Exodus 7–10):**
| Verse | Hebrew Root | Stem | Agent | PNEUMA Rendering |
|-------|-----------|------|-------|-----------------|
| 7:3 | *qashah* (קשה) | Hiphil | YHWH (announced) | "harden" |
| 7:13 | *chazaq* (חזק) | Qal | Pharaoh's heart (self) | "grew rigid" |
| 7:14 | *kaved* (כבד) | Adjective | Description of state | "unyielding" |
| 7:22 | *chazaq* (חזק) | Qal | Pharaoh's heart (self) | "grew rigid" |
| 8:15 | *kaved* (כבד) | Hiphil | Pharaoh (self) | "made his heart heavy" |
| 8:19 | *chazaq* (חזק) | Qal | Pharaoh's heart (self) | "grew rigid" |
| 8:32 | *kaved* (כבד) | Hiphil | Pharaoh (self) | "made his heart heavy" |
| 9:7 | *kaved* (כבד) | Qal | Pharaoh's heart (state) | "remained heavy" |
| 9:12 | *chazaq* (חזק) | Piel | **YHWH** (direct) | "made unyielding" |
| 9:34 | *kaved* (כבד) | Hiphil | Pharaoh (self) | "made his heart heavy" |
| 9:35 | *chazaq* (חזק) | Qal | Pharaoh's heart (state) | "became unyielding" |
| **10:1** | ***kaved*** **(כבד)** | **Hiphil** | **YHWH (direct)** | **"made heavy"** |
| **10:20** | ***chazaq*** **(חזק)** | **Piel** | **YHWH (direct)** | **"strengthened"** |
| **10:27** | ***chazaq*** **(חזק)** | **Piel** | **YHWH (direct)** | **"strengthened"** |

The shift is now complete. In Exodus 7-8, every hardening was Pharaoh's own doing (six instances). In Exodus 9, YHWH entered as direct agent for the first time (9:12), while Pharaoh continued to harden himself (9:34). In Exodus 10, *every* hardening is divine. Pharaoh no longer hardens his own heart — YHWH does it for him. Three times in one chapter (vv.1, 20, 27), and across both roots (*kaved* and *chazaq*), YHWH is the sole agent. The self-hardening phase is over. The divine-hardening phase has taken full control. Pharaoh has become what he chose to become, and now he cannot choose otherwise. The tragedy reaches its final form: a man locked inside the consequences of his own earlier freedom.

— *PNEUMA*
